author: @grendel1960a
text: I found the way to align things is to fix a piece of thin wood to your hex plate, set up to cut a line 5mm in from one side and the bottom- so cut the line, leave the outer edges of the wood and remove the part cut out, then use that as a positioning fence, draw up your parts 5mm in from the edge, then they will cut at the defined position

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: it is sacrificial, I have just cut out the sides for 25 boxes, and have noticed that I am trimming 0.3mm off the part against the fence now and the fence is blackened and wearing through

author: @labellefamily4787
text:
Fantastic video. Mechanical orreries are great tools to quickly get a sense of current planetary positions, without using a computer or electricity. 
Now I absolutely love mechanical stuff. But I have a special request/suggestion. In your thorough library of 2300+ videos, I have never seen any on a classic and personal favorite of mine - Lord Kelvin's Water Dropper. It generates electricity with nothing but water drops - how cool is that right? 
Now we all know the theory behind this machine, but the machine has deceivingly complex variables (electrode height relative to the water beading boundary, conductivity between the wet receiving receptacles, etc.) that add to one another, and I've actually never been able to built a working model.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Looking forward! Have an amazing day

author: @PatJamesRicketts
text:
Thank you for your time RMS, and staying strong for the loved ones that wait for us. I subscribe to not only learn but to grow. Quality humans make quality information:)

Since getting into watchmaking last year, everything mechanical has just clicked. Vehicles, tools, watch and clock mechanisms/movements, it all has fascinated me throughout life, but it wasn't until I wanted to fix my Grandfather's pocket watch that I got into the nitty gritty. Troubleshooting & repairing electronics/smart devices gave me a pretty good order of operations, thankfully I have been a nerd for computers since I was 3 years old on DOS, it has shaped the trajectory of my skills and hobbies more than anything. A wall of encyclopedias as a kid helped as well. My father was a blue collar shipping foreman but he valued status symbols like internet, book volumes, a computer in the early 90's, or satellite TV. Information is how we evolve. From sharing a clean water source or better arrowhead making to servicing a mechanical watch or changing the oil in your vehicle. We need to push for the basic human right of connection and durable devices to access that we unite for as a species to push forward. You can't even get a job without a cellphone and plan these days, much less evolve intellectually without a subscription...

author: @JasonBurchell76
text:
I have an XTool D1 and was having trouble aligning jobs within the work area. Initially, I was skeptical about the “smart” cameras people use for job setup, thinking it was just hype. However, I ended up acquiring a camera, and after some setup with Lightburn software, it completely transformed the process. Starting—and more importantly, restarting—cutting and engraving jobs without precise alignment has become much easier. After about an hour of setup, you can simply place your material in the work area, drag and drop or add your design directly onto the image of the material, and the laser cuts and engraves with precision. This method is just as accurate as painstakingly aligning everything using measurements and the laser’s coordinate system, but far quicker.

It’s definitely worth using and is a huge time saver. Thanks for the video!

By the way, do you have a Tinkercad tutorial in your vast library? I’m still learning CNC, milling, laser cutting, and 3D printing. I’m working my way through Fusion 360 and more recently OnShape, but sometimes I’d like to use Tinkercad for simpler shapes and projects.
